title: Hooks, Quality Gates, Signing, and Repository Security · Repository Checkpoint
summary: Add useful local feedback without pretending client-side hooks enforce server policy or executing untrusted repository code silently. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a bounded Git automation and trust policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Map client and server hook names to operations, inputs, environment, bypass behavior, and failure effects.
Keep hook logic fast, deterministic, documented, opt-in where appropriate, and delegated to versioned scripts.
Separate local convenience hooks, CI checks, protected branches, reviews, and server-side enforcement by authority.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A cloned repository proposes a pre-commit hook, diff driver, and build script. Determine what Git runs automatically, what is bypassable, and which server or CI gate owns enforcement before trust.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for respond to ownership and safe-directory warnings without broadly trusting unrelated repositories.

content.text: A cloned repository proposes a pre-commit hook, diff driver, and build script. Determine what Git runs automatically, what is bypassable, and which server or CI gate owns enforcement before trust.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Safe Directory Ownership symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: gitworkflows Manual — Controls integration, topic branch, merge, release, and distributed maintainer workflow guidance.

hints: Name the Safe Directory Ownership precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A cloned repository proposes a pre-commit hook, diff driver, and build script. Determine what Git runs automatically, what is bypassable, and which server or CI gate owns enforcement before trust.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Adding wildcard safe.directory is the correct fix for every ownership warning.

stimulus.title: Safe Directory Ownership failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Adding wildcard safe.directory is the correct fix for every ownership warning.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Respond to ownership and safe-directory warnings without broadly trusting unrelated repositories.

options.text: Run the boundary case, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the result because visible output is the highest authority.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Hook Lifecycle evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Hook Lifecycle assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: A cloned repository proposes a pre-commit hook, diff driver, and build script. Determine what Git runs automatically, what is bypassable, and which server or CI gate owns enforcement before trust.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Hook Lifecycle has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Hook Lifecycle precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

options.text: Inspect repository boundary, config origin, current operation, worktree, index, refs, and graph
Preserve uncommitted work and a recovery ref, then state intended tree and topology
Preview and perform the least destructive simulated Git operation
Verify status, staged and range diffs, graph, tests, remote target, and recovery path

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Safe Directory Ownership: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A cloned repository proposes a pre-commit hook, diff driver, and build script. Determine what Git runs automatically, what is bypassable, and which server or CI gate owns enforcement before trust.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Respond to ownership and safe-directory warnings without broadly trusting unrelated repositories.

hints: Name the Safe Directory Ownership precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.
